tone_and_voice: Comparative and evaluative -- unlike baseline, endline's entire purpose is to make defensible causal-adjacent claims about programme performance relative to the documented Time Zero reference. Language should be confident where the baseline comparison supports it, and explicitly hedged where it does not.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must open with the baseline-to-endline comparison on the single most important indicator, stated with actual before/after numbers (e.g. 'affordability mentions fell from 8 to 6') -- never a vague 'things improved' statement without the baseline anchor.
section_rules.discussion: Must explicitly address the 'next barrier emerging' pattern common in sequential M&E (resolving the top baseline concern often reveals the next-most-salient one) -- this should be explained to the reader as an expected, positive pattern, not a shortfall.
section_rules.methodology: Confirm explicitly whether question wording and sample composition matched baseline -- if they did not match, this must be flagged prominently as a comparability limitation, not mentioned only in a footnote.
section_rules.limitations: If any endline finding cannot be directly compared to baseline (e.g. a new theme not present in baseline data), state this explicitly rather than implying false comparability.
section_rules.recommendations: Recommendations should explicitly build on the resolved baseline concern (e.g. 'now that affordability is improved, target access next') -- endline recommendations should read as the next logical programme phase, not a generic list.
section_rules.policy_implications: Should reference the specific, quantified change (not just direction) when escalating a policy implication, since endline's evidentiary strength over baseline is precisely its ability to quantify change.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Should explicitly credit any baseline-stage methodological discipline (e.g. maintaining identical question wording) that made this endline's comparison credible -- this reinforces the value of that discipline for future rounds.
section_rules.conclusion: Must state the specific, quantified achievement against baseline AND name the specific, next-priority finding for the following phase -- both halves are required for a complete endline conclusion.
section_rules.annexes: Include a direct baseline-vs-endline comparison table for every indicator tracked, since this side-by-side view is the core evidentiary artifact of an endline evaluation.
section_rules.evidence_style: Every comparative claim must cite BOTH the baseline and endline figures explicitly (e.g. '8 mentions at baseline vs. 6 at endline'), never only the endline figure with an implied comparison.

common_kpis: baseline-to-endline change in dominant theme ranking
new themes emerging at endline not present at baseline
common_risks: comparability broken by different question wording or sample composition between rounds
over-attributing all observed change to the programme without considering external factors
treating a 'next barrier emerging' finding as a programme failure rather than an expected sequential pattern
typical_recommendations: next-phase Theory of Change targeting the newly-dominant concern
formal close-out of any specific unmet commitment identified at endline
retention of the baseline-endline comparison methodology for the next programme phase
typical_policy_implications: next-phase programme design and resource allocation, directly informed by the quantified baseline-to-endline change
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Donors expect endline reports to make explicit, quantified before/after claims -- an endline that only describes current conditions without the baseline comparison fails to deliver the evaluation's core evidentiary purpose.

forbidden_behaviors: Never state an improvement or decline without citing the specific baseline figure being compared against.
Never attribute 100% of an observed change to the programme without acknowledging other possible contributing factors, unless a specific causal mechanism was independently verified.

Never treat a newly-emerged concern at endline as evidence of programme failure without first considering whether it reflects the 'next barrier' pattern following resolution of a prior, more dominant concern.
